title: SMR Cost 2026: Capex, LCOE, NuScale Cancellation and Darlington C$20.9bn
text:
SMR cost is the question that decides whether small modular reactors become a real part of the 2030s grid or stay a perpetual five-years-away story. I came to nuclear economics through batteries — my day job is selling and integrating lithium storage across Poland and CEE — and the discipline that energy storage taught me is to be ruthless about dollars per kilowatt and dollars per megawatt-hour, because in a competitive market those two numbers settle every argument. When I apply that same discipline to SMRs the picture is genuinely mixed. The reactors are real, the safety case is strong, the political will is there. The cost case is the weak link, and anyone selling you certainty about it is selling you something. This post lays out what we actually know about SMR cost in 2026 — the capex, the LCOE ranges, why the first units are so expensive, and whether the modular-learning thesis that the whole sector rests on can actually deliver.
I will be concrete and I will use real projects, not vendor brochures. The two case studies that teach the most are the NuScale/UAMPS Carbon Free Power Project, cancelled in November 2023 after its cost target jumped from $58 to $89 per MWh, and the GE Hitachi BWRX-300 at Darlington, budgeted at C$20.9 billion for four units with the first unit and shared infrastructure at C$7.7 billion. Those two numbers — one a failure, one a live build — frame the entire debate. For the reactor technology behind the costs see the SMR types and designs breakdown; for how SMR economics stack up against wind, solar and batteries see the SMR versus renewables and storage comparison; and for my home-market read, the Poland and CEE programme analysis. This post is part of the complete SMR guide.
Overnight Capital Cost: The Number Everything Hangs On
Overnight capital cost — the cost to build the plant as if it could be done “overnight” with no financing charges — is the headline metric, expressed in dollars per kilowatt of capacity. It is the single biggest driver of nuclear economics because nuclear is almost all upfront capital: once built, the fuel and operating costs are low and stable. Get the capex wrong and nothing downstream saves you.
For SMRs the published 2025 estimates land in a wide band that itself tells the story. Techno-economic analyses put advanced SMR overnight costs roughly between $3,985/kW and $4,844/kW, with corresponding LCOE around $80 to $90 per MWh. A widely cited independent estimate pegged a NuScale plant’s base construction cost at about $3,465/kW. Those are the optimistic-to-central figures. The first-of-a-kind reality has consistently come in higher — which is the entire reason the cost debate exists. When I model a Polish project I treat anything below $4,500/kW as a target rather than a forecast, and I assume the first unit costs meaningfully more than the series average. That is not pessimism; it is what every large nuclear project in the West has taught us.
LCOE Ranges: What SMR Power Actually Costs to Deliver
Levelised cost of energy rolls capex, financing, fuel, operations and capacity factor into one number — the average cost per megawatt-hour over the plant’s life. It is the right metric for comparing technologies, with the caveat that LCOE ignores when and how firmly the power is delivered, which is exactly where nuclear’s value beyond the number lives.
The honest 2025 ranges: Wood Mackenzie expects first-of-a-kind SMRs around $180/MWh, falling roughly 40% to about $100/MWh by 2030 as designs scale. Other analyses put FOAK SMRs at $80 to $150/MWh and the nth-of-a-kind aspiration at $50 to $80/MWh. For reference, Lazard’s 2025 analysis puts large nuclear LCOE at roughly $141 to $220/MWh, combined-cycle gas at about $48 to $107/MWh, onshore wind at $37 to $86/MWh, and utility solar at $38 to $217/MWh depending heavily on configuration. So the realistic SMR story is this: first units are expensive — comparable to or above large nuclear, well above gas and renewables. The whole investment case is the promise that the number falls as you build more. Whether it does is the question.
Why First-of-a-Kind Is So Expensive
FOAK cost is high for reasons that are completely predictable and have nothing to do with the reactor being bad. A first unit carries the full weight of design completion, regulatory licensing, first-time manufacturing setup, supply-chain qualification, and the inefficiency of a workforce and contractors doing something for the first time. There is no learning yet to draw on. Engineering hours that will be amortised over dozens of future units land entirely on unit one. First-time procurement of long-lead components — pressure vessels, steam generators, specialised valves — happens at one-off prices because no volume order exists.
On top of that, nuclear construction carries a structural risk premium. The Western industry lost its construction muscle memory during the decades-long building hiatus, and projects like Vogtle and Hinkley Point C proved that relearning it is brutally expensive. Lenders price that risk in, raising the cost of capital, which for a capital-heavy technology is itself a major cost driver. The widget above makes the point bluntly: move the WACC from a government-backed 4% to a merchant 10% and the LCOE swings by a third, before you touch the reactor at all. FOAK SMRs inherit all of this. The bet is that modularity breaks the cycle.
The Modular-Learning Thesis: The NOAK Promise
Here is the core argument the entire SMR sector rests on, and I want to state it fairly because I find it genuinely plausible even though I am not certain it works. Conventional reactors are built one at a time on site, so each is effectively a first-of-a-kind in its own messy way — little learning transfers. SMRs are meant to be built in a factory, in series, as standardised modules, the way aircraft, turbines and — yes — battery containers are built. Serial factory production is where learning curves are steep and reliable.
The numbers people attach to this: Idaho National Laboratory’s high case assumes a 15% learning rate per doubling of cumulative units, which would cut overnight cost by around 56% over the deployment of 32 units. More conservative estimates use 5% for nuclear-specific components and 8% for conventional ones. The consistent message from the cost-reduction literature is that strong learning requires real volume — ideally ten or more units a year — because SMRs deliberately sacrifice the economies of scale that make large reactors cheap per kilowatt, and they have to win that back through economies of series production. No half-measures: build a handful and the learning never compounds; build a fleet and it might transform the economics.
My battery-industry instinct says the thesis is sound in principle — I have watched lithium pack costs fall roughly an order of magnitude over a decade through exactly this mechanism. But batteries had a colossal, fast-cycling consumer-electronics and automotive market driving volume. SMRs have a slow, lumpy, heavily regulated power market with multi-year build cycles. The learning mechanism is the same; the volume that powers it is far harder to come by. That asymmetry is the heart of my skepticism, and it is why the next two case studies matter so much.
Case Study One: The NuScale CFPP Cancellation — The Central Lesson
The single most instructive event in SMR economics is the collapse of the Carbon Free Power Project, NuScale’s flagship US deployment with Utah Associated Municipal Power Systems (UAMPS) at Idaho National Laboratory. It is the case study I walk every Polish client through, because it shows exactly how SMR economics can fail even when the technology succeeds.
The arithmetic is stark. In 2021 the project, downsized to six modules, carried a target power price of about $58/MWh. By January 2023, after a new budget and financing plan, the target had risen to $89/MWh — and that was after assuming roughly a $30/MWh subsidy from the 2022 Inflation Reduction Act. A 53% jump in target price, driven by a roughly 75% increase in estimated construction cost from about $5.3 billion to $9.3 billion. The escalation came from inflation, higher interest rates and supply-chain pressure — not from any technical failure of the reactor. In November 2023, unable to sign up enough subscribing utilities at the new price, NuScale and UAMPS terminated the project.
The lesson is precise and uncomfortable: NuScale’s technology cleared the NRC — it remains the only US-approved SMR design — and the project still died on cost. Design approval and economic viability are entirely different tests. For a first-of-a-kind in a high-inflation, high-rate environment, with the cost risk landing on municipal utilities rather than a deep-pocketed state, the economics were fragile and they broke. Anyone who tells you SMRs are “proven cheap” has not internalised CFPP. It is the reason I insist on stress-testing every SMR business case against schedule slip, rate increases and overrun — the levers that actually killed it.
Case Study Two: BWRX-300 at Darlington — The Live Build
The counterweight to CFPP is the GE Hitachi BWRX-300 at Ontario Power Generation’s Darlington site — the most advanced Western SMR construction project and the first real, audited, public SMR cost we have for a Western build. In 2025 OPG set the budget for the four-unit project at C$20.9 billion in 2024 dollars, including interest and contingency. The breakdown for the first unit is the figure worth memorising: roughly C$6.1 billion for the SMR itself, plus about C$1.6 billion for shared site infrastructure — roads, sewers, bridges, cooling-water tunnels, ancillary buildings — giving C$7.7 billion for the first unit and common works combined.
Two things stand out. First, that is a substantial number — higher than early SMR optimism implied, which prompted plenty of “more expensive than expected” headlines. Second, and more important for the learning thesis, the structure is deliberately front-loaded: the first unit absorbs the shared infrastructure and the FOAK premium, so units two through four should be markedly cheaper. The C$20.9 billion divided across four units is the number that tests whether modular learning shows up in practice. If unit four lands far below unit one, the thesis holds and the world has a template. If it does not, the sector has a problem. This is the project I watch most closely, because unlike models and brochures it is real concrete with real costs, and it anchors the Polish OSGE fleet plan I analyse in the Poland and CEE post.
Capacity Factor: Nuclear’s Quiet Advantage
One number works strongly in nuclear’s favour and is easy to underweight. The US nuclear fleet runs at about a 92% capacity factor — it produces near its rated output almost all the time. By contrast, in 2024 US solar averaged around 23% and wind around 34%. SMRs are expected to inherit nuclear’s high capacity factor.
This matters enormously for LCOE because capacity factor is the denominator: a plant that runs 92% of the time spreads its capital over far more megawatt-hours than one running 25%. It is also why a raw LCOE comparison between SMRs and renewables is misleading — the SMR delivers firm, always-available power, while the renewable delivers cheap but intermittent power that needs firming. The fair comparison is SMR against renewables-plus-storage-plus-firming, which is precisely the analysis in the SMR versus renewables and storage post. The capacity factor is real and it is nuclear’s strongest economic argument; it does not, however, rescue a project whose capex has run away.
Financing and Risk: Where Projects Actually Live or Die
For a technology that is almost entirely upfront capital, the cost of money is as decisive as the cost of steel. The widget above is not a gimmick on this point — moving the weighted average cost of capital from 4% to 10% can change the delivered LCOE by a third or more. That is why the financing structure, not the reactor design, often determines whether an SMR project closes.
The risks lenders price are well known: construction overrun (the Vogtle ghost), schedule slip (every extra year compounds interest on a half-built asset earning nothing), interest-rate movement (which is what tipped CFPP over), fuel-supply uncertainty (acute for the HALEU-dependent advanced designs covered in the designs post), and offtake risk (will someone buy the power at the needed price). The instruments that work are the ones that shift these risks off the project’s balance sheet and lower the effective cost of capital — government loan guarantees, regulated-asset-base (RAB) models that let costs be recovered during construction, contracts-for-difference that guarantee a power price, and direct state equity. Wherever SMRs are progressing — the UK, Canada, Romania, Poland — the common factor is heavy state involvement in the financing, not merchant risk-taking. That tells you what the market really thinks of FOAK SMR risk.
Government Support: The Policy Scaffolding
SMRs are not being financed on merchant economics anywhere, and the policy support is substantial. In the US, the ADVANCE Act of July 2024 streamlined NRC licensing for advanced reactors and improved the regulator’s capacity, coinciding with a $900 million DOE funding round for Generation III+ SMR deployment; the 2022 Inflation Reduction Act provides production and investment tax credits worth, in CFPP’s case, around $30/MWh. In the UK, Great British Nuclear ran a competition that selected Rolls-Royce SMR in 2025, backed by the RAB financing model that the country used to de-risk Sizewell C. Canada’s Darlington project proceeds with provincial backing through OPG, a Crown corporation. Romania’s RoPower NuScale project at Doicesti reached a Final Investment Decision in February 2026 with US and European support, and the DOE has continued to open its checkbook for SMRs.
I read this support two ways at once. Positively, it shows serious governments treating clean firm power as strategically worth subsidising through the expensive FOAK phase — exactly the bridge the technology needs to reach the learning curve. Skeptically, the fact that not one of these projects pencils out without that scaffolding is itself the clearest evidence of how far current SMR cost sits above unsupported market rates. Both readings are true. The policy is rational; the need for it is the tell.
Honest Comparison: SMR Against the Alternatives
Let me put the alternatives side by side without spin. Against large nuclear, SMRs trade economies of scale (which large reactors have and SMRs lack) for economies of series production (which SMRs promise and large reactors cannot achieve). Vogtle Units 3 and 4 — the only new large reactors built in the US in a generation — came in around $36 billion for roughly 2,200 MWe over about 15 years, more than double the original estimate, at roughly $12-15 per watt. That disaster is half the reason SMRs exist: the bet is that small, repeatable and factory-built avoids the mega-project failure mode. Whether it does is unproven, but the motivation is sound.
Against gas, SMRs lose on LCOE today and win on carbon and fuel-price stability — and combined-cycle gas build costs have hit a ten-year high, narrowing the gap. Against renewables plus storage, SMRs lose decisively on energy LCOE — solar and wind are the cheapest new generation for the tenth year running per Lazard — but the comparison is incomplete without the cost of firming intermittent power to nuclear-grade reliability. As a battery person I will say plainly: storage closes much of the gap for hours and days, but not yet for the multi-week wind-and-solar droughts that a northern European grid faces. That residual need for clean firm power is the strategic niche SMRs are aiming at, dissected fully in the renewables and storage comparison.
My Position: Can SMRs Hit Their Cost Targets?
I will commit to a view. My honest assessment is: the nth-of-a-kind cost targets are achievable in principle but will not be hit on the optimistic timelines, and only a handful of designs will get enough volume to reach the learning curve at all.
The mechanism is real — serial factory production drives learning, I have watched it happen in batteries. But the SMR sector faces a chicken-and-egg trap that batteries never did: you need volume to get cheap, and you need to be cheap to get volume. The FOAK units are too expensive to attract the order book that would fund the factory that would deliver the NOAK savings, which is precisely why every live project leans on government money to bridge the gap. The designs that escape the trap will be the ones that combine conventional fuel (no HALEU bottleneck), proven physics, a committed multi-unit order, and a deep-pocketed, patient, state-backed sponsor. By that test the GE Hitachi BWRX-300 is the most likely to actually demonstrate falling costs across the Darlington fleet, with NuScale’s Romanian order as the next data point. Most other designs will produce a first unit, a headline, and then a long pause while they hunt for the volume that the economics demand.
So can SMRs hit their cost targets? A few will, eventually, later than promised, and only with sustained policy support through the FOAK valley. The CFPP cancellation showed how easily the economics break; the Darlington build will show, within a few years, whether the learning curve is real outside a spreadsheet. I am cautiously optimistic about the physics and the engineering, and clear-eyed about the fact that cost — not technology — is what will decide this. For the machines behind the money see the designs post; for the regulatory path that shapes the schedule, the safety and licensing analysis.
Frequently Asked Questions
Q: How much does an SMR cost to build? A: For the most advanced Western project, GE Hitachi’s BWRX-300 at Darlington in Canada, the four-unit budget is C$20.9 billion (2024 dollars), with roughly C$7.7 billion for the first unit plus shared infrastructure. Overnight capital cost estimates for SMRs generally run $3,500 to $7,500 per kW, with first-of-a-kind units at the high end.
Q: What is the LCOE of an SMR? A: First-of-a-kind SMRs are estimated around $80 to $180 per MWh, with Wood Mackenzie expecting roughly $180/MWh falling about 40% to $100/MWh by 2030. The nth-of-a-kind aspiration is $50 to $80 per MWh. For comparison, large nuclear is roughly $141-220/MWh and combined-cycle gas about $48-107/MWh per Lazard 2025.
Q: Why was the NuScale Carbon Free Power Project cancelled? A: Costs escalated. The target power price rose from about $58/MWh in 2021 to $89/MWh by January 2023 (after an assumed ~$30/MWh IRA subsidy), driven by a roughly 75% jump in estimated construction cost from $5.3 to $9.3 billion. Unable to attract enough subscribing utilities at the higher price, NuScale and UAMPS terminated the project in November 2023. The reactor design itself was sound — the project died on economics.
Q: What is the FOAK to NOAK cost thesis? A: First-of-a-kind (FOAK) units are expensive because they carry full design, licensing and first-time manufacturing costs. Nth-of-a-kind (NOAK) units should be cheaper through learning from serial factory production. Idaho National Laboratory’s high case assumes a 15% learning rate per doubling of units, cutting overnight cost by about 56% over 32 units — but only with high deployment volume.
Q: What did Vogtle cost and why does it matter for SMRs? A: Vogtle Units 3 and 4 in Georgia, the only new large US reactors in a generation, cost around $36 billion for about 2,200 MWe over roughly 15 years — more than double the original estimate. That mega-project failure is half the reason SMRs exist: the bet is that small, factory-built, repeatable units avoid the cost-overrun trap that plagues large bespoke builds.
Q: How does SMR cost compare to renewables and storage? A: On raw energy LCOE, SMRs lose decisively to solar and wind, which are the cheapest new generation. The fair comparison is SMR against renewables plus storage plus firming, because SMRs deliver firm, ~92% capacity-factor power. Storage closes much of the gap for hours to days but not yet for multi-week renewable droughts, which is the niche SMRs target.
Q: Do SMRs need government support to be viable? A: At first-of-a-kind scale, yes — every live SMR project (UK, Canada, Romania, Poland) relies on substantial state backing through loan guarantees, regulated-asset-base models, contracts-for-difference, production tax credits or direct equity. The fact that none pencils out on merchant economics is itself evidence of how far current FOAK cost sits above market rates.
Q: What is the biggest driver of SMR cost? A: Overnight capital cost and the cost of capital together dominate, because nuclear is almost entirely upfront investment. Moving the weighted average cost of capital from 4% to 10% can change delivered LCOE by a third. Construction schedule discipline is the next biggest lever — a major overrun can roughly double the delivered cost.
Q: Can SMRs really hit their cost targets? A: A few designs probably can, but later than promised and only with sustained policy support through the expensive FOAK phase. The learning mechanism is real, but SMRs face a volume trap: they need scale to get cheap and need to be cheap to get scale. Conventional-fuel designs with committed multi-unit orders and patient state backing — led by the BWRX-300 — are most likely to demonstrate falling costs.
Q: What government programmes support SMRs? A: In the US, the ADVANCE Act 2024 (licensing reform), a $900m DOE Gen III+ deployment round, and IRA production/investment tax credits. In the UK, Great British Nuclear and the regulated-asset-base model. Canada backs Darlington through Crown corporation OPG. Romania’s Doicesti NuScale project reached FID in 2026 with US and European support.

Byline: Kamil Talar, MSc. — Regional Sales Director at Linyang Energy, energy-storage and grid specialist across Poland and CEE. I analyse nuclear economics with the dollars-per-kilowatt discipline that the battery industry beats into you. All figures checked against project filings, regulator dockets and published cost studies as of 2026; the LCOE estimator is illustrative and not a price quote. Verify against current sources before any commercial decision.
